senses.definition: stupid or slow to understand; not quick-witted
examples: He made a slackwitted comment that showed he hadn’t thought it through.
The quiz exposed how slackwitted he was about basic math facts.

senses.definition: the quality of being slackwitted; deficiency in quick understanding
examples: Her slackwittedness often led to missed cues in conversations.
The textbook explains why slackwittedness can hinder learning.

senses.definition: performing symbolic or superficial online actions to support a cause without real-world impact
examples: Many view slactivism as easy virtue signaling rather than genuine commitment.
They criticized slactivism when the campaign needed on-the-ground volunteers.

senses.definition: plural of slactivism; acts or instances of performative online activism
examples: The article cataloged several slactivisms seen during the week.
Critics debate the impact of various slactivisms on public policy.

senses.definition: a person who engages in slactivism; someone who supports a cause mainly through online gestures
examples: Some slactivists never volunteer in real life but post about causes online.
The panel discussed how slactivists influence public opinion.

senses.definition: plural of slactivist; people who engage in slactivism
examples: The report criticized several slactivists for not backing up their online posts with real actions.
Slactivists were invited to join the town-hall meeting.

senses.definition: obsolete or dialect word for a type of lath or wooden rod; or a South African mining term; not common in modern standard English
examples: The old text mentions a slad used in carpentry.
In some dialects, slad could refer to a slender stick.

senses.definition: a Malaysian/Malay term referring to a small plantation or farm, often palm or other crops; used in local contexts
examples: The farmer took care of the sladang with care.
sladang workers organized a harvest.

senses.definition: plural of sladang; multiple farms or plantations in Malay contexts
examples: The sladangs stretched across the valley.
Investment flowed into several sladangs near the coast.

senses.definition: a shallow open valley or hollow; or a surname/place name; British place-name element
examples: The cottage stood at the edge of the slade.
Slade Manor is a local landmark.

senses.definition: plural of slade; valleys or referential proper nouns formed from the same root
examples: The two slades in the map are connected by a stream.

senses.definition: obsolete plural of slade; rarely used in modern English
examples: The glossary lists slae as an archaic term.

senses.definition: obsolete form; plural of slae or historic variant; not common in contemporary use
examples: Historical texts mention slaes in the same way as slade.

senses.definition: the waste left after smelting ore; a coarse material; also a slang insult referring to a person perceived as worthless
examples: The furnace produced slag.
Don't listen to that slag of a remark.

senses.definition: to criticize or belittle someone; to act in a despicable way
examples: They slagged him for his mistakes.
Stop slagging off your teammates.

senses.definition: the quality of being slaggable; capable of forming slag or of being slagged in humor or insulting context
examples: The comedian tested the slaggability of the crowd with sharp jokes.

senses.definition: able to be slagged or capable of being insulted or criticized (informal, humorous)
examples: The crowd is particularly slaggable tonight.
That remark made him feel slaggable in front of everyone.

senses.definition: past tense of slag; to criticize or belittle; or to remove slag from ore
examples: They slagged the speaker for hours.
The ore was slagged to separate impurities.

senses.definition: a person who slags; someone who criticizes or belittles others; or a tool used for slagging in industry
examples: The slagger at work was infamous for harsh critiques.
A slagger in the workshop cleaned the slag from pipes.

senses.definition: more slaggy; more likely to be insulted or to insult others; in slang sense, more insulting
examples: That remark was slaggier than the previous one.
Watch out—the jokes get slaggier as the night goes on.

senses.definition: most slaggy; most insulting or most associated with slag in humor or criticism
examples: That was the slaggiest joke of the night.
They delivered the slaggiest lines with swagger.

senses.definition: the act of slagging; insulting or criticizing someone; or the process of removing slag in industry
examples: He faced a lot of slagging after the release.
The slagging of the mineral is part of the refining process.

senses.definition: plural of slagging; instances of slagging or criticisms
examples: There were several slaggings during the debate.

senses.definition: resembling slag; coarse or dirty in texture; slangy/dirty in humor
examples: The slaggy slaggy ore clumped together.
That was a slaggy joke.

senses.definition: free from slag; without slag; clean or pure in industrial sense
examples: The slagless sample passed quality tests.
A slagless surface is easier to recycle.

senses.definition: the state of being slagless; absence of slag
examples: Slaglessness indicates purity in the sample.
